refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback) over sustained operational periods (≥1 hour continuous). Document network conditions and loss rate; confirm ≤1% or customer-defined target.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of lost packets on command and feedback integrity.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, prioritization) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall provide operator and safety manager visibility into communication quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency, link status).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (primary + backup) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure impact on packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management with dynamic packet prioritization, compression, and retry logic tuned to network conditions.
DP-C: Integrate network monitoring and telemetry collection to measure packet loss in real-time and trigger operator alerts and safety interventions when thresholds are exceeded.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: adaptive QoS + real-time monitoring with feedback loop to safety services and operator interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement an integrated teleoperation communication stack combining (1) adaptive QoS management with dynamic packet prioritization, compression, and retry logic tuned to agreed network conditions, and (2) real-time packet loss monitoring with automated alerts to Safety Services and operator feedback when loss exceeds customer-defined threshold.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation communication logic (adaptive QoS) with safety alerting logic (threshold monitoring → SafetyAlert). This is acceptable because packet loss is inherently a safety-critical quality attribute; decoupling would require redundant monitoring and risk inconsistent safety responses.
DP-D depends on accurate network condition characterization (latency, jitter, bandwidth) to tune QoS parameters. Coupling to network characterization is necessary and manageable via configuration.
DP-D requires bidirectional data flow: telemetry (vehicle → operator) and tasking (operator → vehicle). This is inherent to teleoperation and not a design coupling issue.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is justified by safety criticality. Recommend clear interface contracts between adaptive QoS module and Safety Services to minimize hidden dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will provide or agree on representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, loss baseline) for tuning QoS parameters.
ASSUMPTION: Operator and Safety Manager will accept automated alerts and interventions triggered by packet loss thresholds.
RISK: If network conditions vary significantly beyond agreed envelope, adaptive QoS may not maintain ≤1% loss; mitigation requires dynamic re-tuning or fallback to manual operator override.

RISK: Real-time monitoring adds computational and communication overhead; if not carefully designed, monitoring itself could increase packet loss; mitigation requires lightweight telemetry collection and asynchronous reporting.
RISK: Retry logic and compression may introduce latency; if latency exceeds operator tolerance, safety may be compromised; mitigation requires latency budgeting and operator training.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) QoS tuning parameters (compression ratio, retry count, priority queue depth) as configuration inputs tied to agreed network conditions, (2) packet loss threshold for SafetyAlert trigger (default ≤1%, customer-configurable), (3) latency budget for retry logic (e.g., max 200ms round-trip), and (4) monitoring overhead cap (e.g., telemetry ≤5% of link bandwidth).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication stack for remote equipment operation, including adaptive QoS management, packet loss monitoring, and integration with Safety Services and operator feedback. Scope includes command transmission (Tasking), telemetry reception (Telemetry), safety alerting (SafetyAlert), and network link management (Network Link).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) transmitted from operator console to vehicle. Teleoperation module prioritizes Tasking packets and applies QoS logic to ensure timely delivery.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state, sensor data, and feedback transmitted from vehicle to operator console. Teleoperation module monitors Telemetry packet loss and reports metrics to operator and Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts triggered when packet loss exceeds threshold or communication link degrades. Teleoperation module sends SafetyAlert to Safety Services for operator notification and potential intervention (e.g., emergency stop, mode restriction).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (wireless, cellular, satellite, or hybrid). Teleoperation module adapts QoS parameters based on Network Link characteristics (latency, jitter, bandwidth, loss baseline) and monitors link health.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse teleoperation communication logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) without clear ownership. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side hardware and control logic. Packet loss monitoring and adaptive QoS require bidirectional visibility (operator ↔ vehicle). Assigning DP-D solely to Vehicle would split communication logic between Vehicle and operator console, creating diffusion. Vehicle should own vehicle-side telemetry collection and link monitoring, but not the full adaptive QoS stack.

Teleoperation module is explicitly designed to own end-to-end remote operation communication. DP-D (adaptive QoS + packet loss monitoring) is a core teleoperation function. Teleoperation naturally owns Tasking input, Telemetry output, Network Link management, and SafetyAlert integration. High containment, minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting. Packet loss monitoring is a safety input, but the communication stack itself (QoS, retry logic, compression) is not a safety service function. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication engineering with safety policy. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the communication logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of DP-D because: (1) it owns the end-to-end communication path (Tasking in, Telemetry out, Network Link management), (2) adaptive QoS and packet loss monitoring are core teleoperation engineering functions, not safety policy or vehicle control, (3) containment minimizes diffusion of communication logic across Vehicle and operator console, and (4) Teleoperation has clear interfaces to Safety Services (SafetyAlert) and operator feedback without embedding safety logic.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it owns both communication stack and operator interface logic. Mitigation: clearly separate communication layer (QoS, monitoring) from operator UI layer; use well-defined internal interfaces.

RISK: If Vehicle module independently implements packet loss detection or retry logic, diffusion occurs. Mitigation: establish clear contract that Vehicle reports raw link metrics to Teleoperation; Teleoperation owns adaptive QoS decisions.

RISK: Safety Services may need to override or bypass Teleoperation QoS logic in emergency scenarios (e.g., force emergency stop regardless of packet loss). Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a high-priority interrupt channel; Teleoperation must guarantee SafetyAlert delivery even under degraded network conditions.

RISK: Network Link characteristics may vary dynamically (e.g., switching between cellular and satellite). Teleoperation must dynamically re-tune QoS parameters. Mitigation: implement configuration update mechanism and test re-tuning under realistic network transitions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign DP-D to Teleoperation module with the following clarifications: (1) Teleoperation owns the adaptive QoS stack (compression, retry, prioritization) and packet loss monitoring logic. (2) Vehicle module provides raw link metrics (packet count, loss count, latency samples) to Teleoperation via a well-defined telemetry interface. (3) Teleoperation sends SafetyAlert to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds threshold; Safety Services owns the policy response (e.g., operator notification, mode restriction). (4) Operator console receives communication quality metrics from Teleoperation via a dedicated dashboard or HMI interface (not explicitly listed as a known interface, but implied by operator feedback requirement). (5) Document the latency budget and monitoring overhead cap as design constraints for Teleoperation implementation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (adaptive QoS + real-time monitoring with SafetyAlert integration) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (maintain packet loss ≤1% under agreed network conditions). The DP provides a logical solution path: adaptive QoS reduces packet loss by prioritizing critical commands and retrying lost packets; real-time monitoring detects when loss exceeds threshold and triggers safety interventions. No independence axiom violation: the DP is a single, coherent solution concept. Coupling is justified by safety criticality.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If Vehicle module independently implements packet loss recovery (e.g., local retry logic), and Teleoperation also implements retry logic, redundant and conflicting logic may emerge. Mitigation: establish clear ownership boundary; Vehicle reports metrics, Teleoperation owns QoS decisions.

Diffusion risk: If operator console implements its own packet loss monitoring (separate from Teleoperation), inconsistent alerts may confuse operator. Mitigation: Teleoperation is the single source of truth for packet loss metrics; operator console displays Teleoperation-provided metrics.

Diffusion risk: If Safety Services implements its own packet loss threshold logic (separate from Teleoperation), SafetyAlert may be triggered inconsistently. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns threshold logic and SafetyAlert generation; Safety Services owns policy response.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) for DP-D. The module owns all four known interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and the adaptive QoS + monitoring logic fits naturally within teleoperation scope. Minimal diffusion expected if ownership boundaries are clearly documented and enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via Teleoperation module: (1) Teleoperation can measure end-to-end packet loss by tracking Tasking and Telemetry packets across the Network Link. (2) Teleoperation can report packet loss metrics to test harness or operator console for measurement. (3) Acceptance test can run teleoperation under agreed network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth) and confirm packet loss ≤1%. (4) Teleoperation's SafetyAlert output can be verified to trigger when loss exceeds threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for maintaining packet loss ≤1% through adaptive QoS and for alerting Safety Services when loss exceeds threshold. Vehicle module is responsible for providing accurate link metrics. Safety Services is responsible for responding to SafetyAlert (e.g., operator notification, mode restriction). Operator console is responsible for displaying communication quality metrics. No ambiguity in responsibility allocation.

recommended_revisions: Refine the acceptance criterion to specify the measurement methodology: e.g., 'Packet loss measured as (lost packets / total packets) over a 1-hour continuous teleoperation session under agreed network conditions, with ≤1% loss rate.'
Document the agreed network conditions as a configuration input to Teleoperation module (e.g., latency 50-200ms, jitter ±20ms, bandwidth 1-10 Mbps, baseline loss 0-0.5%).
Define the SafetyAlert trigger logic explicitly: e.g., 'SafetyAlert triggered when packet loss rate exceeds 1% over a 10-second rolling window, or when Network Link status changes to degraded.'

Establish a design review checkpoint to validate that Teleoperation's adaptive QoS parameters (compression ratio, retry count, priority queue depth) are tuned for the agreed network conditions and do not introduce unacceptable latency.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (detection, error recovery, adaptation, visibility). DP-D is a coherent solution concept, but its realization requires detailed design of adaptive QoS algorithms, packet loss detection logic, and SafetyAlert integration. Teleoperation module is the correct IM boundary with high containment and minimal diffusion. The FR-DP-IM chain passes all gates. Recommend proceeding to the next decomposition level to refine sub-FRs and allocate them to sub-modules or design components within Teleoperation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize impact of lost packets on command and feedback integrity.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, retry logic, prioritization) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall provide operator and safety manager visibility into communication quality metrics (packet loss rate, latency, link status).

assumptions: Customer will provide or agree on representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, loss baseline) for tuning QoS parameters.
Operator and Safety Manager will accept automated alerts and interventions triggered by packet loss thresholds.
Vehicle module will provide accurate link metrics (packet count, loss count, latency samples) to Teleoperation.
Network Link is stable enough to support adaptive QoS tuning (i.e., network conditions do not change faster than QoS adaptation can respond).
Latency budget for retry logic is acceptable to operator (e.g., max 200ms round-trip).
Monitoring overhead (telemetry collection and reporting) is ≤5% of link bandwidth.
open_risks: If network conditions vary significantly beyond agreed envelope, adaptive QoS may not maintain ≤1% loss; mitigation requires dynamic re-tuning or fallback to manual operator override.
Real-time monitoring adds computational and communication overhead; if not carefully designed, monitoring itself could increase packet loss.
Retry logic and compression may introduce latency; if latency exceeds operator tolerance, safety may be compromised.
If Vehicle module independently implements packet loss recovery, redundant and conflicting logic may emerge.
If operator console implements its own packet loss monitoring, inconsistent alerts may confuse operator.
If Safety Services implements its own packet loss threshold logic, SafetyAlert may be triggered inconsistently.
Emergency scenarios may require Safety Services to override Teleoperation QoS logic; SafetyAlert delivery must be guaranteed even under degraded network conditions.
evidence_needed: Agreed network conditions document (latency, jitter, bandwidth, loss baseline) signed by customer.
Teleoperation module design specification with adaptive QoS algorithm details, packet loss detection logic, and SafetyAlert integration.
Latency budget analysis showing that retry logic and compression do not exceed operator tolerance.
Monitoring overhead analysis showing that telemetry collection and reporting is ≤5% of link bandwidth.
Test plan for packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions with acceptance criteria (≤1% loss over 1-hour session).
Interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle module specifying link metrics format and update frequency.
Interface contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services specifying SafetyAlert format and trigger conditions.
Operator console design specification showing communication quality metrics display.
Design review checkpoint results validating QoS parameter tuning for agreed network conditions.
